summary: This advice column installment covers four reader questions spanning text editors, probability puzzles, topological data analysis, and nerd stereotypes. Aunt Pythia defends emacs while questioning the 'macho crap' around vim's keyboard-only approach. She provides a detailed visual solution to the two-daughter probability problem, arriving at 13/27. On topological data analysis, she shares her own related idea about finding holes in data manifolds. She concludes by apologizing for reinforcing stereotypes about nerds and sports while coining terms 'nerock' and 'jord' for athletic nerds.
key_points: The preference for keyboard-only text editors like vim is questioned as unnecessary machismo rather than genuine efficiency gain
The two-daughter probability problem (with Friday birth condition) has a precise answer of 13/27, solvable through careful bin-counting in a 14x14 grid
Topological data analysis could potentially identify impossible outcomes by finding 'holes' in high-dimensional data manifolds
Stereotyping nerds as necessarily bad at sports or other activities is unfair and 'fundamentally un-nerdy'
money_quotes: As an aside, I'd like to argue this point, because it's often shrouded in weird macho crap: why not use your mouse?
I honestly have never been prevented from coding efficiently because my arm is too tired from moving from the keyboard to the mouse and back.
Is the goal really to be able to stay in the exact same position for as long as possible?
Geez I don't know about you but my rear end is not topologically equivalent to my coffee cups. You either need to go to a doctor or buy some coffee cups that don't leak.
Although, statistically I believe I still have a point, there's no reason to limit people in arbitrary ways like that, and it's fundamentally un-nerdy of me to do so.
But just for the record, nerds are categorically excellent at sex. We all know that. Say yes.
key_insight: Even in playful advice-column format, rigorous thinking matters—whether solving probability puzzles with careful enumeration or questioning the unstated assumptions behind cultural preferences like text editor choices.
